elements.sent-2411-001: We propose to design atomtronic circuits with Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs) in circuit-like traps that are controlled via mobile barriers.
elements.sent-2411-002: Using classical-field simulations, we demonstrate a universal set of logical gates and show how to assemble them into circuits.
elements.sent-2411-003: We first demonstrate an AND gate based on a T-shaped BEC, utilizing a combination of mobile and static barriers.
elements.sent-2411-004: The mobile barriers provide the logical input of the gate, while the static barrier functions as a Josephson junction that generates the AND output of the gate via a density imbalance across the barrier.
elements.sent-2411-005: Next we show how to combine three AND gates into a circuit, with a design composed of two T-shapes and an H-shape.
elements.sent-2411-006: Furthermore, we demonstrate how to use Josephson oscillations to create a NOT gate and combine it with an AND gate, thereby showcasing a universal set of gates and their assembly into circuits.
elements.sent-2411-007: Introduction The emergent technology of atomtronics utilizes the substantial achievements of cold-atom technology to emulate electronic circuitry \cite{Amico2021, Amico2022, Polo2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Bose-Einstein condensates (BECs), due to their low susceptibility to decoherence and dissipative effects, are particularly well-suited for studying superfluid phenomena in ultracold quantum systems \cite{Raman1999, Onofrio2000, Dalibard2000, KetterleVortex, Greiner2002,Hadzibabic2006, Salomon2014, Weimer2015, Shin2018, Pfau2018, Murthy2015, Sunami2022, Sunami2024, Rydow2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-009: This has inspired the design of systems that closely resemble superconducting circuit elements \cite{Seaman2007,Stickney2007,Pepino2009,Pepino2010,Amico2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: A fundamental component in superconducting circuits is the Josephson junction (JJ), typically formed by a superconductor-insulator-superconductor interface, which gives rise to the dc-ac Josephson effect \cite{Josephson1962 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: This effect produces a supercurrent across the junction, entirely controlled by the phase difference between the two superconductors.
elements.sent-2411-012: When the current exceeds a critical current, the junction transitions into a resistive state, associated with the creation of elementary excitations \cite{barone1983josephson .}
elements.sent-2411-013: In cold-atom systems, atomic JJs have been realized with static or mobile tunnel junctions, drawing close parallels to their superconducting counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-014: This has enabled the measurement of current-chemical potential and current-phase relations \cite{Levy2007, Kwon2020, Luick2020, Pace2021 , and has further led to the development of atomic analogs of superconducting quantum interference devices (SQUIDs) \cite{Ramanathan2011, Wright2013, Ryu2013, Eckel2014, Ryu2020, Kiehn2022 .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: A notable extension of this analogy is the recent observation of Shapiro steps in cold-atom systems \cite{singh2023shapiro, Del_Pace2024, Bernhart2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: In this paper, we propose an experimental methodology to realize a universal set of logical gates using atomic JJs , and their assembly into circuits.
elements.sent-2411-017: We begin by constructing a two-input AND gate using a T-shaped condensate, which includes two mobile and one static tunnel barrier.
elements.sent-2411-018: The mobile barriers generate external atomic currents, while the accumulated density imbalance across the static barrier functions as the circuit's output.
elements.sent-2411-019: When only one barrier is moved, no significant imbalance is observed.
elements.sent-2411-020: However, when both barriers are moved simultaneously, a substantial imbalance is produced, demonstrating the successful operation of the atomic AND gate.
elements.sent-2411-021: To expand this approach, we design a circuit for a four-input AND gate by combining two T-shaped condensates into an H-shaped configuration.
elements.sent-2411-022: We demonstrate the protocol for manipulating the barriers, ensuring that a significant imbalance occurs only when all four input barriers are moved simultaneously.
elements.sent-2411-023: For all other input combinations, the resulting imbalance is small, as expected for a four-input AND gate.
elements.sent-2411-024: Finally, we demonstrate the operation of a NOT gate using a single static barrier in a rectangular-shaped cloud, where Josephson oscillations are used to flip the input state.
elements.sent-2411-025: With the implementation of both an AND gate and a NOT gate, we have a universal set of logical gates that can be combined to perform any classical logical operation, as demonstrated by the realization of a NAND gate.
elements.sent-2411-026: The results are obtained using a classical-field approach, which incorporates fluctuating bosonic fields to capture dynamics beyond the mean-field approximation \cite{Blakie2008, Polkovnikov2010, Singh2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: This paper is structured as follows: In Sec.
elements.sent-2411-029: Simulation Method We consider a 2D bosonic cloud spatially confined in trap geometries that are designed to support atomtronic operation.
elements.sent-2411-030: To simulate the dynamics we use a classical-field method within the truncated Wigner approximation \cite{Singh2016, Singh2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: The system is described by the Hamiltonian \begin{equation}\label{eq:hamContinuum} \hat{H}_0 = \int d\vec{r}\bigg[ \frac{\hbar^2}{2m} \vec{\nabla} \hat{\psi}^\dagger (\vec{r}) \vec{\nabla} \hat{\psi} (\vec{r}) + \frac{g}{2} \hat{\psi}^\dagger (\vec{r})\hat{\psi}^\dagger (\vec{r})\hat{\psi} (\vec{r})\hat{\psi} (\vec{r})\bigg],\end{equation} where $\hat{\psi ^\dagger(\vec{r )$ and $\hat{\psi (\vec{r )$ are the creation and annihilation field operators, respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-032: The interaction is given by $g = \sqrt{8\pi a_s \hbar^2/ (l_z m)$, where $a_s$ is the s-wave scattering length, $l_z = \sqrt{\hbar/(m\omega_z)}$ is the harmonic oscillator length in the transverse direction, and $m$ is the atomic mass.}
elements.sent-2411-033: While we present our results for the concrete realization provided by $^6\text{Li _2$ molecules, our proposed circuits can be realized with any cold-atom degenerate gas.}
elements.sent-2411-034: We use $\tilde{g =0.1$, the 2D density $n\approx 2.3\,\mu \text{m ^{-2}$ and the temperature $T/T_0=0.1$, with $g=\tilde{g \hbar^2/m$ and $T_0 = 2\pi n\hbar^2/(m k_B D_c)$ being the estimate of the critical temperature based on the critical phase-space density $D_c = \ln(380/\tilde{g )$ \cite{Prokof_ev_2002, PhysRevLett.87.270402 .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: To perform numerical simulations, we discretize space on a lattice of size $N_x \times N_y$ with a discretization length $l = 0.5\, \mu \mathrm{m}$.
elements.sent-2411-036: The value of $l$ is chosen to be larger than both the healing length $\xi = \hbar / \sqrt{2mgn}$ and the de Broglie wavelength, ensuring that the continuum limit is satisfied \cite{PhysRevA.67.053615 .}
elements.sent-2411-037: This procedure maps the continuum Hamiltonian onto the discrete Bose-Hubbard model, where the tunneling energy is defined as $J = \hbar^2/(2ml^2)$ and the on-site interaction energy as $U=g/l^2$.
elements.sent-2411-038: In our classical-field representation we replace the operators $\hat{\psi}$ in Eq. \ref{eq:hamContinuum and in the equations of motion by complex numbers $\psi$.}
elements.sent-2411-039: The initial states $\psi(\vec{r ,t=0)$ are sampled in a grand canonical ensemble with chemical potential $\mu$ and temperature $T$ via a classical Metropolis algorithm.}
elements.sent-2411-040: Finally, each initial state is propagated using classical equations of motion \begin{equation}\label{eq:eom} i \hbar \partial_t \psi_j = - J \sum_{i(j)} \psi_{i(j)} + V_ext(\br_j, t) \psi_j + U |\psi_j|^2 \psi_j,\end{equation} where $V_{\text{ext ({\bf r ,t)$ is the external barrier potential.}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: The site $i(j)$ refer to the four neighboring sites $i$ of site $j$.
elements.sent-2411-042: To create atomtronic logical gates we use combinations of static and mobile barriers of the form \begin{equation}V_\ms(r,t) = V_{0, \ms}(t) \exp{\bigg(-\frac{2 (r- r_{0, \ms})^2}{\sigma_{\ms}^2}\bigg)}, \label{eq:BarrierPotentialStat} V_\mm(r,t) = V_{0, \mm}(t) \exp{\bigg(-\frac{2 (r- r_{0, \mm}- v t)^2}{\sigma_{\mm}^2}\bigg)}. \label{eq:BarrierPotentialMob}\end{equation} As we describe in detail below, $V_0(t)$ is the time dependent strength, $\sigma$ is the width, and $r_0$ is the initial position of the barrier.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}V_\ms(r,t) = V_{0, \ms}(t) \exp{\bigg(-\frac{2 (r- r_{0, \ms})^2}{\sigma_{\ms}^2}\bigg)}, \label{eq:BarrierPotentialStat} V_\mm(r,t) = V_{0, \mm}(t) \exp{\bigg(-\frac{2 (r- r_{0, \mm}- v t)^2}{\sigma_{\mm}^2}\bigg)}. \label{eq:BarrierPotentialMob}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-043: For the mobile barrier, $v$ represents its velocity.
elements.sent-2411-044: We define the scaled parameters $\tV_0=V_0/J$ and $\tsigma=\sigma/l$.
elements.sent-2411-045: We linearly ramp up $V_0(t)$ to the desired value over $150$ ms, then allow the system to relax for $50$ ms to ensure equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-046: These ramp-up and waiting times remain fixed throughout the paper, unless stated otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-047: The inclusion of barriers creates weak links at $r_{0,\ms}$ and $r_{0,\mm}$, see, e.g. \cite{Singh2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-048: To generate an atom current, we move the mobile barrier at a constant velocity $v$ until it reaches the final position $r_f$ \cite{Smerzi1997 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: The mobile barriers create currents, which function as input channels, while the density imbalance at static barriers serves as output channels.
elements.sent-2411-050: The behavior of these channels depends on the critical dynamics across the weak links, which form atomic JJs.
elements.sent-2411-051: Each proposed circuit element is designed based on a specific barrier protocol, as described in the following sections.
elements.figure-2411-002: \text{(a)} [-1em] \begin{minipage}[c]{0.45\linewidth} \begin{circuitikz}[scale=1] \draw (0,0) node[and port] (myand) {} (myand.in 1) -- ++(-0.25,0.0) node[left] {A} (myand.in 2) -- ++(-0.25,-0.0) node[left] {B} (myand.out) -- ++(0.25,0) node[right] {Out}; \end{circuitikz} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}[c]{0.35\linewidth} \begin{tabular}{c|c|c} \,\,\,$v_A$\,\,\, \,\,\,$v_B$\,\,\, $z(t)$ $0$ $0$ $0$ $0$ $v_0$ $0$ $v_0$ $0$ $0$ $v_0$ $v_0$ $> 0$ \end{tabular} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}[c]{1.\linewidth} \text{(b)} [-0em] \includegraphics[width=0.98\columnwidth]{imbalance_plot_all_sets.pdf} \caption{ Implementation of an atomtronic AND gate. (a) Sketch of a standard AND gate is shown alongside the corresponding truth table for the atomtronic AND gate. $v_A$ and $v_B$ represent the velocities of the individual barrier movements, while $z(t)$ denotes the density imbalance across the static barrier. (b) Time evolution of the imbalance $z(t) $ is shown after the completion of the barrier movements; see text. When only the barrier A is moved or only the barrier B is moved, the imbalance remains relatively small. However, a significant imbalance is observed when both barriers A and B are moved together. } \label{fig2} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-052: Two-Input AND Gate In this section, we demonstrate the realization of an atomtronic AND gate in a 2D homogeneous T-shaped BEC, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig1 .}
elements.sent-2411-053: The system consists of a closed design with dimensions $L_x \times L_y = 200 \times 120 \,\mu\text{m ^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-054: The AND gate acts as a circuit element exhibiting transistor-like behavior, which we implement by introducing three Gaussian barriers in each arm of the reversed T-shaped BEC.
elements.sent-2411-055: Two of these barriers, labeled as A and B, are mobile and positioned at the edges of the condensate at $x_{0, \mm =0 \,\mu\text{m}$ and $y_{0, \mm =120\,\mu\text{m}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: In our proposed operation each of these barriers is moved either at zero velocity or at a velocity $v_0$.
elements.sent-2411-057: This binary choice encodes the input of the logic gate.
elements.sent-2411-058: These barriers are sufficiently high to not allow tunneling across them.
elements.sent-2411-059: Therefore, they act as classical pistons and induce currents as they push the atomic superfluid.
elements.sent-2411-060: The third, static barrier in the lower right part of the inverse-T shape, has a barrier height that allows for tunneling across the barrier.
elements.sent-2411-061: Therefore it acts as a Josephson junction on the superfluid dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-062: In particular, it displays a critical current behavior.
elements.sent-2411-063: If the current across the junction is smaller than it, the density imbalance across it remains small.
elements.sent-2411-064: However, if the current is larger than the critical current, significant density imbalance builds up, corresponding to the resistive regime.
elements.sent-2411-065: This was used to determine the current-chemical potential relationship in atomic JJs \cite{Kwon2020, Pace2021 , where the finite chemical potential regime occurs via vortex emission \cite{Burchianti2018, Xhani2020, singh2023shapiro .}}
elements.sent-2411-066: For barriers A and B, we use $\tV_{0, \mm =3$, $\tsigma_\mm=8$ and equal displacement of $\Delta x\, = \Delta y = 50\,\mu \text{m}$.}
elements.sent-2411-067: The velocity is chosen to be $v=v_0 = 0.1$ mm/s.
elements.sent-2411-068: This velocity induces a current at the static barrier that is below the critical current, if only one of the mobile barriers is moved.
elements.sent-2411-069: We refer to the barrier velocity that creates the critical current as $v_c$, so we have $v_0 < v_c$.
elements.sent-2411-070: The static barrier is positioned at $x_{0, \ms = 130\,\mu\text{m}$ in the right horizontal arm, with $\tV_{0, \ms =2.5$ and $\tsigma_\ms=3$.}}
elements.sent-2411-071: The key mechanism to create an AND gate, or a transistor-like behavior, is that if both barriers A and B are moved at $v_0$, the resulting total current exceeds the critical current, producing a density imbalance at the static barrier, see Fig. \ref{fig1 (a).}
elements.sent-2411-072: In contrast, when either A or B is moved independently at $v_0$, the junction remains in the subcritical regime, allowing supercurrent tunneling, and no density imbalance appears at the static barrier, see Figs.
elements.sent-2411-073: To quantify these observations, we calculate the imbalance across the static barrier as \begin{equation}\label{eq:imbalance} z(t) = \frac{n_\text{left}(t) - n_\text{right}(t)}{n},\end{equation} where $n_\text{left (t)$ and $n_\text{right (t)$ represent the densities in the left and right reservoirs across the static barrier, here determined within the region $ 80\,\mu\text{m < x < 180\,\mu\text{m}$ and $ 0\,\mu\text{m < y < 40\,\mu\text{m}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-074: In Fig. \ref{fig2 (a) we show the time evolution of $z(t)$ after the movement of the barriers for three different input configurations.}
elements.sent-2411-075: When either A or B moves alone, no imbalance is observed.
elements.sent-2411-076: However, when both A and B move together, a nonzero imbalance is created.
elements.sent-2411-077: These results are summarized in the table in Fig. \ref{fig2 (a), which corresponds to the logic table of an AND gate.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Circuit of AND gates Having implemented a logical AND gate in Sec.
elements.sent-2411-079: Specifically, we expand the 2-input AND gate to a circuit of AND gates to create a 4-input AND gate, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig3 .}
elements.sent-2411-080: For this, we connect two T-shaped structures, each providing two inputs, within an H-shaped configuration that serves as the designated output.
elements.sent-2411-081: The H-shaped connector couples to the regions located to the left and right of the static barriers that were previously analyzed for the density imbalance in the 2-input AND gate.
elements.sent-2411-082: For both T-shaped circuits we use the same parameters as in the previous section.
elements.sent-2411-083: The barrier protocol for the 4-input AND gate is shown in Fig. \ref{fig3 (d).}
elements.sent-2411-084: In this setup, a static barrier is placed within the H-shaped structure at $x_{0, \ms =130 \,\mu \mm$, with parameters $\tV_{0, \ms =2$ and $\tsigma_\ms = 2$.}}
elements.sent-2411-085: This new barrier is slightly weaker than the two static barriers used in the top and bottom parts of the setup.
elements.sent-2411-086: The areas adjacent to this new barrier now constitute the output of the 4-input AND gate circuit, specifically, whether a density imbalance builds up between them, or not.
elements.sent-2411-087: Furthermore, we introduce four Gaussian barriers, two left and two right output gates, at $y=120\,\mu\text{m}$ and $y=180\,\mu\text{m}$, connecting the top, bottom, and middle regions.
elements.sent-2411-088: The parameters for these gates are $\tV_{0, \ms =5$ and $\tsigma_\ms = 3$.}
elements.sent-2411-089: These barriers prevent particles from tunneling through prematurely, ensuring that the initial AND-gate protocols complete without interference, and that the generated output remains unaltered.
elements.sent-2411-090: At $t=650$ ms, after both 2-input AND gates in the top and bottom area have completed their operations, the mobile barriers are in their final positions.
elements.sent-2411-091: We then linearly ramp down the right output gates from $\tV_{0, \ms =5$ to $0$ over a period of $200 \,$Ms. After this step, a waiting period of $50$ ms allows the system to stabilize.}
elements.sent-2411-092: Finally, we gradually open the left output gates over $150\,$ms, followed by another $50$ ms waiting time to allow the system to settle.
elements.sent-2411-093: In Fig. \ref{fig3 , this process is depicted for the case of moving all input barriers, resulting in a nonzero imbalance across the new static barrier.}
elements.sent-2411-094: For comparison, an example resulting in zero imbalance is discussed in Appendix \ref{sec:appA .}
elements.sent-2411-095: We assign the circuit output based on the density imbalance created across the output barrier within the area of $ 80\,\mu\text{m < x < 180\,\mu\text{m}$ and $130\,\mu\text{m < y < 170\,\mu\text{m}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-096: We calculate the imbalance $z$ according to Eq. \ref{eq:imbalance .}
elements.sent-2411-097: In Fig. \ref{fig4 (b), we show the time evolution of $z(t)$ for all cases, up to symmetric combinations.}
elements.sent-2411-098: As a key result we observe that only in the case where the barriers A, B, C, and D are moved simultaneously, a clear, nonzero density imbalance emerges, corresponding to a logical $1$.
elements.sent-2411-099: All other cases generate near-zero imbalance corresponding to a logical $0$.
elements.figure-2411-004: \text{(a)} [-2em] \begin{minipage}[c]{1.\linewidth} \begin{circuitikz} \draw (0,0) node[and port] (myand1) {} (myand1.in 1) -- ++(-0.5,0.0) node[left] {A} (myand1.in 2) -- ++(-0.5,-0.0) node[left] {B} (0,-1.5) node[and port] (myand2) {} (myand2.in 1) -- ++(-0.5,0.0) node[left] {C} (myand2.in 2) -- ++(-0.5,-0.0) node[left] {D} (2.5,-0.75) node[and port] (myand3) {} (myand1.out) -- (myand3.in 1) (myand2.out) -- (myand3.in 2) (myand3.out) -- ++(0.3,0) node[right] {Out}; \end{circuitikz} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}[c]{1.\linewidth} \text{(b)} [-0em] \includegraphics[width=0.98\columnwidth]{Circuit_Results_Imb.pdf} \caption{Results of the logical atomtronic 4-input AND gate. (a) Sketch of a 4-input AND gate. (b) Time evolution of the density imbalance $z(t)$ is shown for all relevant cases among the 16 possible combinations of the 4 mobile input barriers (A, B, C and D). In brackets are the cases that behave similarly due to the symmetry of the setup.} \label{fig4} \end{minipage}
elements.figure-2411-007: \begin{minipage}[c]{0.45\linewidth} \begin{circuitikz} \draw (6,0) node[nand port] (mynand) {} (mynand.in 1) -- ++(-0.35,0) node[left] {A} (mynand.in 2) -- ++(-0.35,0) node[left] {B} (mynand.out) -- ++(0.35,0) node[right] {Out}; \end{circuitikz} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}[c]{0.45\linewidth} \begin{tabular}{c|c|c} \,\,\,$v_A$\,\,\, \,\,\,$v_B$\,\,\, $z(t)$ $0$ $0$ $> 0$ $0$ $v_0$ $> 0$ $v_0$ $0$ $> 0$ $v_0$ $v_0$ $ 0$ \end{tabular} \end{minipage} \caption{Sketch of a standard NAND gate and the corresponding truth table for the atomtronic NAND gate. $v_A$ and $v_B$ denote the velocities of the individual barrier movements. $z(t)$ represents the final imbalance determined after the phase imprint. } \label{fig:NOT/NAND-gate Symbol + Table}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[height=7.4cm]{BEC_AND4_Gate_h_h_650.pdf} \includegraphics[height=7.3cm]{BEC_AND4_Gate_h_h_900.pdf} \includegraphics[height=7.3cm]{BEC_AND4_Gate_h_h_1095.pdf} \caption{Dynamical regimes of an atomtronic circuit. (a) Density distribution $n(x,y)$ is shown at time $t=650\text{ ms}$, after only the horizontal barriers B and C are moved together at a constant velocity of $v_0= 0.1 \text{ mm/s}$ from the edges of the condensate to their final positions. Each two-input AND gate consists of two mobile barriers with heights $\tV_{0, \mm}=3$ and widths $\tsigma_\mm= 8$, and one static barrier with a height $\tV_{0, \ms}=2.5$ and width $\tsigma_\ms= 3$ (orange dashed lines). Four Gaussian barriers, each with a height of $\tV_{0, \ms}=5$ and a width of $\tsigma_\ms = 3$, separate the two-input AND gates from the central part, functioning as left and right output gates. A static barrier with a height of $\tV_{0, \ms}=2$ and width $\tsigma_\ms= 2$ (white dashed lines) is placed in the central part, acting as the output channel. (b) At $t=900\text{ ms}$, $n(x,y)$ is shown after the right output gates are opened. (c) At $t=1095\text{ ms}$, $n(x,y)$ is displayed after the left output gates are opened. } \label{fig3:Dynamical Regimes of an atomtronic 4-input AND-gateEXTRA}
elements.sent-2411-100: Universal Set of Gates In the previous sections, we have demonstrated how a T-shaped BEC can be utilized to create an atomtronic AND gate, and how these gates can be operated sequentially to create circuits.
elements.sent-2411-101: Now, we extend this concept by constructing a logical NOT gate.
elements.sent-2411-102: To achieve this, we create an atomic JJ by separating a 2D cloud into two subsystems using a static tunnel barrier.
elements.sent-2411-103: This approach is inspired by experiments that observe Josephson oscillations, triggered by an instantaneous phase imprint on one of the subsystems \cite{Luick2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-104: We use a rectangular cloud with dimensions $L_x\times L_y = 40\times 40\,\mu \text{m ^2$.}
elements.sent-2411-105: The tunnel barrier has a height of $\tV_{0, \ms =1.5$ and a width of $\tsigma_\ms =3$.}
elements.sent-2411-106: At time $t_\text{phase}$, we imprint a phase of $\phi_0=0.25\,\pi$ on the left subsystem, introducing a phase difference between the two subsystems.
elements.sent-2411-107: This phase difference excites Josephson oscillations in the density imbalance between them.
elements.sent-2411-108: In Fig. \ref{fig:NOT/NAND-gate Symbol + Table (b) we display $z(t)$ after the phase imprint, for half a Josephson oscillation (JO).}
elements.sent-2411-109: During the first quarter of the JO, the imbalance grows from zero to a maximum value, representing the NOT-gate operation where the input is zero and the output is nonzero.
elements.sent-2411-110: In the second quarter of the JO, the imbalance reverses, representing the NOT-gate operation where the input is nonzero and the output is zero.
elements.sent-2411-111: These results are summarized in the table in Fig. \ref{fig:NOT/NAND-gate Symbol + Table (a).}
elements.sent-2411-112: This NOT-gate implementation is fully compatible with the 2-input AND gate.
elements.sent-2411-113: By combining the NOT gate with the AND gate, we create a complete set of logical gates, allowing us to realize a NAND gate.
elements.sent-2411-114: For this purpose, we use the same system parameters as in Sec.
elements.sent-2411-115: The barrier protocol proceeds as follows: We first run the 2-input AND-gate protocol similarly to Sec.
elements.sent-2411-116: Next, to define the NOT-gate area, we introduce two additional static barriers, each with a height of $\tV_{0, \ms =5.0$ and a width of $\tsigma_\ms = 3$, at $x_{0, \ms =125\,\mu \mm$ and $x_{0, \ms =165\,\mu \mm$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: During this period, the original static barrier is ramped up again, increasing from $\tV_{0, \ms =2.5$ to $\tV_{0, \ms =5$, restricting the output to a smaller region within the NOT-gate area.}}
elements.sent-2411-118: After the waiting period, this static barrier is ramped down to $\tV_{0, \ms =1.5$ over $50$ ms, which is the barrier height for the NOT gate.}
elements.sent-2411-119: At this point, we imprint a phase of $\phi_0 =0.25 \, \pi$ on the left subsystem to generate the desired output for the NAND gate.
elements.sent-2411-120: In Fig. \ref{fig:NAND , we show three cases of input barriers before and after the phase imprint.}
elements.sent-2411-121: As expected, the density imbalance observed in Fig. \ref{fig:NAND (a)â€”when both barriers are moved in the AND gateâ€”is no longer measurable in Fig. \ref{fig:NAND (d) after applying the NOT-gate protocol.}}
elements.sent-2411-122: However, if only one barrier is moved [see Figs.
elements.sent-2411-123: After the phase imprint, a nonzero imbalance appears in Figs.
elements.sent-2411-124: We summarize the results of the NAND gate in Fig. \ref{fig:NOT/NAND-gate Symbol + Table .}
elements.sent-2411-125: Conclusions In conclusion, we have demonstrated how to create atomtronic circuits out of Bose-Einstein condensates.
elements.sent-2411-126: As a first example and building block of circuits, we have demonstrated how to use a T-shaped condensate to create an AND gate.
elements.sent-2411-127: The logical input of the gate is given by whether or not Gaussian barriers are pushed into two of the three arms of the T-shaped condensate, to create currents along them, in a piston-like fashion.
elements.sent-2411-128: To create a logical output of the gate, we place a static tunnel barrier in the third arm of condensate.
elements.sent-2411-129: The velocities of the two input barriers is chosen such that if both are moving simultaneously, the current at the tunnel junction is larger than the critical current.
elements.sent-2411-130: As a result, a density imbalance builds up.
elements.sent-2411-131: However, if only one of the barriers is moving, the induced current is below the critical current, and thus no density imbalance builds up.
elements.sent-2411-132: Therefore, the density imbalance across the static barrier serves as the logical output of the AND gate.
elements.sent-2411-133: Going beyond a single gate, we have demonstrated how to operate AND gates in sequence, to create an atomtronic circuit.
elements.sent-2411-134: Furthermore, we have put forth a proposal of how to create a NOT gate in a condensate.
elements.sent-2411-135: For this, we utilize Josephson oscillations.
elements.sent-2411-136: Specifically, we propose to use a quarter of a Josephson oscillation, which transforms a maximal density imbalance into no imbalance, and no imbalance into a maximal imbalance across the tunnel junction.
elements.sent-2411-137: With these circuit elements, AND gate, NOT gate, and how to operate these gates in sequence, we have demonstrated the construction of a NAND gate, thus providing a universal set of logic gates capable of performing any classical logical operation.
